# Poisson with two factors

Real surveys rarely vary one thing at a time. Suppose fish counts depend on both
**habitat** (e.g. reef vs seagrass) and **season** (e.g. wet vs dry). We can put
both factors in the same Poisson model.

## The model

$$
\log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta_A\,A_i + \beta_B\,B_i
$$

Each factor contributes its own 0/1 indicator (its own design-matrix column):

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    count[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta_a * A[i] + beta_b * B[i]
  }
  alpha  ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta_a ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta_b ~ dnorm(0, 10)
}
```

- $\alpha$ — log mean count for the **reference combination** (both factors 0).
- $\beta_A,\ \beta_B$ — multiplicative effects of each factor, **holding the
  other constant**.

This is the additive (no-interaction) model. If you suspect the effect of one
factor depends on the other, you would add an interaction term — but start
simple.

## Your task

Fit the two-factor Poisson model to `fish-counts.csv` and check your posterior
means for $\alpha$, $\beta_A$, and $\beta_B$.
